critic_raw:
This is a carefully constructed experimental design with genuine attention to temporal leakage, label construction, and confound control. Most of the obvious failure modes have been anticipated. I identified three substantive concerns worth raising, one of which is significant enough to threaten validity.

**Issue 1: Ticket-history features embed a lookahead signal relative to the label construction window**

The claim: ticket-history features (ticket count, severity, resolution time for prior 14 days on the same host/user/IP) capture historical risk and contain no future information.

Why this might be wrong: the label for an alert is positive if it is linked to an incident-response ticket within 24 hours. If alert A at time T is labeled positive because it gets linked to ticket X at T+6h, and alert B at time T-8h (same host) is also part of the same incident, then at labeling time B may already have ticket X in its history — meaning B's ticket-history features count a ticket that was opened *because of* A. More concretely: the 14-day lookback for ticket-history features at evaluation time (production) will not include tickets opened by the very incident being triaged, because those tickets don't exist yet. But during *training*, the features are computed from the static historical record, which does include tickets opened after the alert's timestamp, if those tickets were opened within the 14-day lookback window as computed from the feature-extraction date rather than the alert timestamp. This is a subtle but plausible construction artifact: if ticket-history extraction is done in batch (at experiment time) rather than point-in-time (as of alert timestamp), alerts that preceded a major incident will spuriously show high ticket activity in their feature vectors — exactly when they should rank high. The methodology says 'prior 14 days' but does not specify whether the anchor for 'prior' is the alert timestamp or the feature extraction date. If the latter, this is a systematic label-correlated lookahead.

Evidence either way: re-extract ticket-history features using strict alert-timestamp anchors and compare model performance. A large drop in ticket-history feature importance or overall nDCG after fixing the anchor would confirm contamination. The 'metadata leakage check' mentioned in Section 6 partially addresses this but only if it explicitly tests point-in-time correctness of the feature anchor.

**Issue 2: The entity graph is described as 'static' but covers the prior 30 days — this creates a temporal dependency that may differ between training and production**

The claim: entity graph features are extracted from 'a static entity store (host–user–IP–rule relationships from the prior 30 days),' implying temporal correctness.

Why this might be wrong: 'static entity store' is ambiguous. If the graph is rebuilt at experiment time from the full 24-month record and then subgraphs are extracted using a 30-day lookback anchored to the alert timestamp, this is correct. But if 'static' means the graph is built once from all available data and then used as a lookup, then alerts near the beginning of the dataset will have subgraphs enriched with entity relationships that did not exist at alert time (relationships formed later in the 24-month window). This would inflate the information available to training examples but not to production queries, creating a train/production mismatch. The magnitude of the effect scales with graph density and the rate of entity relationship churn in the SOC environment.

Evidence either way: compare entity graph feature distributions between months 1–3 (training, low graph history) and months 21–24 (test, rich graph history). Systematic enrichment differences suggest static graph construction is not point-in-time correct. A model that performs worse early in the training period despite similar alert distributions would be another indicator.

**Issue 3: nDCG@10 as the primary metric assumes a consistent query boundary (shift or session), which the design does not define**

The claim: nDCG@10 aligns with the operational objective because analysts review ~10 alerts per shift.

Why this might be wrong: nDCG@10 is computed per query, and a 'query' in L2R is a ranked list. The design does not specify how alerts are grouped into queries. Options include: per shift, per day, per analyst, per rule category, or per alert-arrival batch. If alerts are grouped in ways that do not correspond to the actual analyst review session (e.g., daily batches when analysts review in 4-hour shifts), then nDCG@10 may not measure the right thing — an alert ranked 8th in a day-batch might be outside the analyst's practical review window if they only see 10 alerts per 4-hour shift and there are multiple shifts per day. The methodological choice here is invisible in the write-up, but it determines what nDCG@10 actually measures.

This is less severe than Issue 1 because it affects interpretation of the absolute nDCG@10 value, not the relative ranking of the proposed model vs. baseline (both are evaluated under the same query grouping). It does not threaten validity of the comparative claim unless the query grouping systematically interacts with the feature groups (e.g., entity graph features are more useful within-shift than across-shift). Worth acknowledging but not a fatal flaw.

**Summary**: Issue 1 is the most significant — a plausible construction artifact that would produce the appearance of ticket-history feature value when in fact the features are contaminated with post-alert ticket activity. The methodology's own 'metadata leakage check' could catch this if it explicitly verifies point-in-time anchoring, but the description does not confirm this. Issues 2 and 3 are moderate and minor respectively. Issue 1 alone warrants flagging as a potential validity threat.
all_issues_raised: Ticket-history features may be anchored to feature-extraction date rather than alert timestamp, causing post-alert ticket activity to appear in pre-alert features during training but not in production — a systematic label-correlated lookahead.

The 'static entity store' description is ambiguous: if the entity graph is built once from the full 24-month record rather than reconstructed point-in-time, early training alerts will have subgraphs enriched with relationships that did not exist at alert time.
The query boundary for nDCG@10 is not defined, making it unclear whether the top-10 window corresponds to an actual analyst review session or an arbitrary grouping.
all_issues_adjudicated: Ticket-history features may be anchored to feature-extraction date rather than alert timestamp, causing post-alert ticket activity to appear in pre-alert features during training but not in production — a systematic label-correlated lookahead.

The 'static entity store' description is ambiguous: if the entity graph is built once from the full 24-month record rather than reconstructed point-in-time, early training alerts will have subgraphs enriched with relationships that did not exist at alert time.
The query boundary for nDCG@10 is not defined, making it unclear whether the top-10 window corresponds to an actual analyst review session or an arbitrary grouping.
